speaker: interviewer
text: London, England. And this is tape eight. OK. Where were we? We were talking about the, the girl who came to help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. And, and then I, I-- anyway, after awhile, I, I just couldn't-- I went. And a social worker never came out to see me who was supposed to come out to see me. And it was a situation was such that I just felt I couldn't continue, or I needed some help. And I went into town, but I collapsed on the street. And I couldn't continue. So I lost this job. And this job, I had permission to work in. But I had no permission to work in any other job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from then on, I, I-- first of all, I was treat-- I had treatment for this [INAUDIBLE]. And, and I had treatment-- and for my heart trouble. And I had treatment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then also, I started getting work, taking work, accepting any illegal jobs. Now, one, two-- my first two jobs were with weavers. The first one was with a weaver who was, who was working on his own with one weaving loom. And then-- and I had to do bobbins, by-- just on one, one wheel, two bobbins for eight hours a day. I was obviously-- because I was working illegally-- I was very, very badly paid. And when he started taking advances, making advances and wanted some other services as well for his money, then I, I went. I, I opted out. And then I got a job with another weaver who had machines. And there were lots of people working there. And what I had to do, I had to make knots, every time a knot, the thread. And I wasn't very good at it. And I found that I was pretty bad at it. So I dropped out of that one, too. And in between, I found-- I got into two choirs. Because I was a very good sight reader. And I got into two choirs. So I earned a bit of money. But I-- all that, I all did this, all this illegally. And I earned a bit of money with that. Then I tried my hand also at some sewing, but sewing by piece in a sweatshop. And I was hopeless. And one day, I couldn't finish a shirt. So I earned absolutely nothing. And, and I came home crying. Now, in between, I was eating in a canteen run by the [INAUDIBLE]. And I also, there was a-- there was a possibility to get into the Sorbonne without certificates, to, to get into the Sorbonne, And I got in. I wanted to do psychopedagogy. And, and I had to take another course, which was a secondary course, which was music aesthetics and appreciation-- not appreciation-- theory and aesthetics and criticism, music criticism also. And in the meantime, I got a job at the Hungarian house, writing out the addresses. But this job was from 2:00 to, to 12:00. And my course in psychopedagogy was in the afternoon. So I couldn't attend that course. And in the meantime, I met-- I, I, I ate in this canteen, this joint canteen. And I always heard people singing. And so I wanted to join a choir. I thought, well, I would love to sing. I didn't know it was a professional choir. But anyway, I went in. And I asked if I could get a audition. They would take me-- took me, they took me on. And, and then one day, there was no choir practice. And, and the, and, but, but I didn't-- and all the people didn't come. And they were notified, but I wasn't notified. And when I came, then the conductor said that there is-- he wanted me to meet someone. And that was Stephan Deak, who was a Hungarian violinist, unemployed without working permission, and in a pretty bad state-- very, very poor, and in a very bad state. And somehow-- I don't know-- I mean, he didn't-- until then, from the time that I had this-- that I was put into prison with the-- from the one man I ever slept with, I never accepted from any man a cup of coffee or anything. I didn't go out with them. I-- people invited me out. I never, never accepted. I absolutely refused. But he was so-- first of all, he knew quite a lot of my friends from Budapest, from the whole circle that I knew and I moved in in Budapest. Then he, he-- he didn't want to go to bed with me straight away. And, and somehow, we started going out together. And he was in that terrible state, but I always thought that because he was hungry and everything. And eventually, we decided that one, one room is cheaper than two rooms, and that we are going to get married. We didn't have-- I mean, I should have run. When I went to his studio, I should have run. Because all the papers were on the f-- I mean, the way it looked, it was so filthy and everything. And I should have run. But I didn't run. I felt sorry for him, because he was in even worse state. And he lost his parents and everybody. And so I, I didn't run. And we eventually got married. I mean, it's too long to tell how. And the whole wedding night, he [? wept ?] through. And that's when I realized that he was not, not quite right, that he was a sick man. And he, then he-- it turned out later, and that I only found out 15 years later-- that he actually was a schizophrenic, and that he already had treatment. He had already electroshock treatment before that, during the war. He was in hiding there for awhile, but that he had some electroshock treatment, that he was a sick man. And so anyway, we were married. And then he, he couldn't get a job. And he wanted to go back to Hungary. And I didn't want to go back to Hungary. And eventually, there was an audition for a South-- a job in South Africa. And he eventually got the job. And when he got the contract, he said that he's schizophrenic. And he can't go. He's mad. And he can't do it. And I spoke to a fr-- a friend who was a psychiatrist. And he said, you have two choices. Either you keep through with him here and you get him-- we get him into mental hospital. But since you haven't got a penny to your name, they won't treat him. And they will-- you will never get there-- from out of there. Or you try and get him onto the boat. He had a priority passage to go to South Africa-- if I get him onto the boat. It's your decision. Well, I decided that I give him a chance, this chance, and get him onto the boat. And so-- but he did all kinds of things. He did suicide attempts, one after the other, but always make sure that he shouldn't succeed. And one of them was-- and that, that was when I had enough-- when he, during the night, he started to threaten me. I woke up that he was trying to kill me. Because then they would kill him. Then they would kill him. And then I moved out. And I moved, moved somewhere else. I came back. I had a job by that time. I was already working at the American Joint Distribution Committee as a filing-- as a [INAUDIBLE] first, and then-- anyway, very badly paid. We-- I just earned enough for one person to live on badly. But for two people, it was really pretty desperate. And we-- I was constantly hungry again and was really in a very bad state. And, and also, I, I was terrified. And I've moved out. I didn't sleep there anymore. But I came back and did his, did his cooking and did his washing. And he-- eventually, I got him on to the boat to South Africa. And then he went to South Africa. And he broke down on the boat completely again. So when he arrived in South Africa, they gave him-- he couldn't take quite a job. But he could play, could play. And they gave him a, a, a black student to look after him, to shave him, to wash him and bring him to the, to the rehearsal or to the concert. And then when-- once he sat down, he played beautifully. So he was all right in that way. They could use him. But-- and then he made-- he told stories about me, that his wife is coming out, who was in Auschwitz and whose nails were torn out. And all kinds of stories, he, he started telling about me. And I didn't want to go. I started my divorce proceedings. I didn't, I didn't want to go. But I was persuaded by the Joint. Everybody-- the Joint was closing down slowly. Slowly, they said, there is no future for you there. You have no working permit. Otherwise, you have no future here. Everybody is, is trying to get out. And they are happy if they have a visa. You have got a visa. You must take it. And they persuaded me that they get me a job. And there's a job lined up for me in Cape Town. I don't need to stay in Durban, where my husband was. And I already started divorce proceedings. And anyway, I eventually went. That's another story, a very long story, how I got there. But I got there. And when I arrived in March, in April, beginning of April, '48, then I, I had a temporary residence permit, which they, they promised me that they are going to change it after three weeks into a permanent residence permit. And then I can go to Cape Town and work in Cape Town. And I had a job lined up in a nurse-- Jewish nursery School for me to work in a Jewish nursery school. And then I found that I had to stay the three weeks in Durban. And then General Smuts fell. And the nationalist government came in. And my temporary residence permit expired. And my, my permanent residence permit didn't come. And I was there without a piece of paper, without residence permit, without working permit, with a husband who was in a terrible state, with whom I really-- with whom I, I-- I mean the Jewish community paid for me to stay somewhere else. Because I didn't want to-- I refused to stay with him. And, and then I, I-- and I found I couldn't get new permission to go to Cape Town. There was no way they'd let me go Cape Town. I had to stay in Durban, because the visa I had, I had only through this job. And event-- and while I was there, I-- he came to visit me. And every day, he got a little bit better. And because I saw the he is getting so much better through me, I somehow-- eventually, we moved in together again. And I became pregnant very soon after. And on my fifth month of pregnancy-- and he became better and better, until he became a genius, and a Superman, and a god, and then complete breakdown. And he had to go into mental hospital. And eventually, he to be certified. And I had to-- I, I was there without a working permit, without a residence permit, with a baby. And, and I was at the end of my tether. I couldn't help him anymore. If I stayed there, I-- they would have sent him to me to, to-- every time he got a little bit better. And I just couldn't take it anymore. I didn't dare to let him near the child, because I was afraid he does something to that child, especially, as when the child was born, he expected a daughter. And he, he couldn't, he couldn't accept it that it was a son. And therefore, I was terrified letting the child alone with him even for a minute, because I didn't know what he was going to do. And, and so I, I went to Israel. I got the Jewish community to pay my trip for Israel. And I said, well, I would pay back. But I go to Israel with a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I-- just one thing that's crossed my mind. You said in, that in Auschwitz, I think it was, that your periods stopped. When did your periods come back again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Much later-- after, long after, I, I-- perhaps in prison in Marseilles.

speaker: interviewer
text: A number-- a few years later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps in prison in Marseilles. Not few years, no-- that was, well, that was-- that would have been '46, no '45, '45 until October.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a year later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that. I think so, but it might have been even later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, then I, I went to Israel with a child. I-- the child, I had to put into a [INAUDIBLE] baby home. And I was in-- because I could only get lodging in a hostel, where I had to share the room with four-- three other women. And I was not allowed to take my child with me. So he was in a baby home. And I was in a hostel. And I was very lucky I got into an [INAUDIBLE], very first [INAUDIBLE], which was as at an experimental stage. But they needed nursery school teachers. So they let me in on, on it. But they couldn't use me without the language. And then I had-- I was bitten by a louse. And I had typhus. I had the spot fever, which is through a louse. And I nearly died. And Ilan had-- my son, Ilan, had meningitis. And I eventually-- well, I eventually got it. I, I, I couldn't work with children anymore. Because there was polio epidemics in Jerusalem. And we weren't allowed to visit. We were allowed to-- and he was on the third floor. And they brought him out on to the, onto the, veranda, onto the balcony. But I was on the, on the ground floor. And I could only wave, wave to him. And that went on. And I found it unbearable. And I just felt I couldn't work with children. And I made an exam. I did, did some evening classes. And I made an exam. And I got into the Minister of Finance as a filing clerk and worked there and worked my way up, and eventually got a so-called flat. And I thought I could bring him home quite soon. The flat were two ex-prison cells with barred, with barred windows, and iron doors, and, and, and scorpions, and no light, and no, no water. But otherwise, it was perfect. And eventually, I got light and water. And then I thought I could get Ilan in. And then came the news that my husband, who-- I had a, a letter from the hospital where he was that he is incurable. And suddenly, he recovered. During an insulin [INAUDIBLE], he suddenly recovered. And, and he was playing again in the orchestra. But he was very upset that we weren't there. Now, I wasn't going to live with him anymore. That was over. I, again, started my divorce proceedings in Israel. But I said, well, he is the father of the child. And I took six months' leave. And I let my flat. And I went to South Africa with Ilan. And I found that he was very happy. And I wanted to go back, except that the person I, I sold-- I, I let my flat to who was an old [INAUDIBLE] friend from [INAUDIBLE] sold my flat. And I would have had to go back to the [INAUDIBLE] again, to, to the hostel again, and live with three other women and put the child into a home. And I decided I'm not doing it. So I couldn't go back to Israel. And  my heart broke, because I was really-- I was just starting to get onto my feet in Israel. And so I didn't go back. I got a-- and then my, my permanent residence permit came through. And I could work. And I got a job at the nursery school, at the Jewish nursery school in Durban. And I started building up things. And my husband-- and we got together. We didn't live together really. But we moved together. We were in the same-- I run his household. And, and the child was-- the child had parents. And then I-- and I was working as a nursery school teacher. And eventually, I was asked to go and help out at the infant school. And then I was asked to stay at the infant school. And from there, I was kicked up to do-- actually, up to A level standard, which I didn't want to d. Because I said, well I haven't done mine. I haven't got my A levels.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the headmaster said, why don't you do it? And I'm hopeless at exams. And my two pupils who were-- who were-- who learned everything from me came out with honors. And I managed to scrape through, which is-- and then I-- but I was-- I didn't want to stay in South Africa.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't stand the apartheid. And I didn't like to be in South Africa. I just didn't want to be in South Africa. And so we tried, but we didn't-- couldn't get out. We had no papers. We could get out of-- I had in between a Hungarian passport for one year. But it was a false passport. I can't tell. It's too long to tell the story how I got it. But I got it through my father, through somebody knowing my father, the ambassador in Paris knowing my father. But it-- when it expired, it wasn't renewed. They told me, you, can't consider yourself Hungarian. And I couldn't get a passport. I had no paper to, to get out, to go somewhere else. I wanted to be nationalized South African to be able to get out, to get a passport and get out. And because my husband did various things, we just couldn't get it. And it took nine years to get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, '57, 1957, we got it. But I was decided that eventually, I'm going to leave him, that I don't, don't want to stay. And we came to England.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here I started-- I found that my qualifications weren't recognized anywhere. So I started as a writing addresses at advanced [? laundry ?], and doing ledger clerk and doing all kinds of thing, any job that I could get. And he got into the philharmonic orchestra and worked there for quite a long time. And I, I eventually started working at [INAUDIBLE] Library. But I found that this was no life. I just couldn't stay with him. I mean, we had no life anyway together anymore. I was just running his house. So I didn't want to stay with him. My son always had to be in a boarding school, because I-- they couldn't be together. They hated each other to such an extend. They never could build up a relationship. My son was so terrified of him. He never touched him, but he was so terrified of him that he couldn't-- so I always, he always have to send away. Because I just didn't earn enough to support both of us. And so anyway, I eventually-- Ilan, and Ilan was incredibly bright. He was brilliant. And at 17, he got to university. And it-- and at 26, he became a-- he became professor at Zurich University as a geneticist. And he would have-- this year, he would have had the Nobel Prize, because two people with whom-- who started working on his project got the Nobel Prize. But anyway, at the age of 30, he committed suicide. And before that, he had, at the age of 21, he had an-- his first affair with a young woman who was older than he was and who-- and there, there is a child from that relationship, which lasted three weeks. After three weeks, he didn't want to go out with her anymore. Then she came that she's pregnant. She said she was on the pill. And she wasn't. And then there is a son. There was the son whom my son never want to see. But I-- from the beginning on, I was grandmother for-- granny to him. And I'm still granny to him. He's now in Oxford and is doing his doctorate. And I hope so. I don't know. He's very, very bright. And he has a master's degree. And he's 24-- has a, has a degree and a master's degrees. And he's doing now his doctorate. And then he married a French girl. And he became-- and he had a-- and he had a daughter in '77, and in '79-- whom he absolutely adored. But his wife hated Zurich and left with the child. Because she just couldn't get accustomed to Zurich. He was a workaholic. He had little time for her. And she went back to Paris. And, and in '79, he committed suicide. And-- I mean, they were constantly in touch. Every week, he went down for the weekend. But anyway, and in the meantime, I, I decided that I must do something about my life. Because I really was very near a complete breakdown. And I decided I either get-- I didn't want to live on my own. Either I find another husband, or I perhaps I try and get a degree. And I, I, I went to lonely clubs every single night. I gave myself two years to find another husband. And after one and a half years, I met this husband. And it became very soon we got together. And a year later, I had my divorce. And we are now married 20-- well, we were together one year without being married and then 25 years. We just had our silver wedding in October. And in between them-- and that was an in between-- these two children were born. And we were grandparents to them. And we still are. And my ex-husband is in a-- had breakdowns, one breakdown after the other. And he was out in Napsbury Hospital. By then, I managed to get him in. He was better. I managed to get him in into one of the Jewish refugee homes. He's in [INAUDIBLE] House, which is one the homes, ref-- Jewish refugee old, old age homes. And I still look after him. And I mean, I still do things for him. And, and when he needs something, I do it. And they phone me if he needs something. Sometimes, he has a breakdown and has to go out to mental hospital again. And I think that's it. I don't know. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: That kind of brings us up to date, doesn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That does bring us up to date, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you married Franz--

speaker: interviewee
text: I perhaps just should say that I worked-- I, I eventually got a job at the [INAUDIBLE] library, where I worked for four and a half years with material on the Holocaust mainly. And then I, I got a job with the [INAUDIBLE] Institute for [INAUDIBLE] endeavors, Overseas Development Institute, which was the only time-- I worked there twice, two periods-- the only time when I've worked in a non-Jewish institute. I loved it, by the way. It was wonderful. It was highly interesting. And then I, I got a job at University College, London. I have no qualifications whatsoever. I tried to get a librarianship qualification. But after my first exams, when I have gained just-- when my teacher thought I was his best pupil and I managed to scrape through-- I decided that I had enough. And I eventually was appointed-- I was in charge of all the Jewish material at University College, two libraries plus an, plus an archive, plus helping out with other Jewish archives, and was recognized as a fully-qualified university librarian without having a piece of paper. And that's how I finished off eight years ago. And then I sat down and wro-- started writing my book.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said before about when you were looking for a hus-- gave yourself two years to look for a husband that things were in awful state for you. And yet now, this number of years on, you seem together and-- what, what caused the-- you know, what helped things to improve for you? Was it the relationship--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to, to, to meet Franz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it being married?

speaker: interviewee
text: To meet Franz yes, certainly yes-- because that, I, I saw things very-- I, I felt that everything was hopeless, that my life was always-- every time I got a little bit up, then bang, I got down again. I had to start again. And I mean, the finer points I didn't bring in, because I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop there.
